description_long: This guard stone is a weathered, upright monolithic structure carved from a single block of stone, mounted on a rectangular base. The front features a partially eroded human figure, possibly of ancient Egyptian style, with a headdress and skirt, though the arms and facial details are heavily worn. The sides reveal the depth of the carving and the rough, unfinished texture of the stone, with visible chipping and surface irregularities. The back is less detailed, showing the broad, angular outline of the headdress and shoulders, with significant surface erosion and pitting. The stone's color is a pale beige with subtle variations, and the surface is matte and rough, indicating exposure to the elements over time. This object would serve as a robust physical barrier, both decorative and functional, in an urban or historical setting.
description: A weathered stone guard stone with a partially eroded human figure on the front, mounted on a rectangular base and finished in a pale beige, rough-textured stone.
description_view_0: Side view showing the depth of the carving, rough stone texture, and the profile of the headdress and body.
description_view_1: Front view displaying the eroded human figure with a headdress and skirt, mounted on a rectangular base.
description_view_2: Opposite side view highlighting the profile of the figure and the uneven, chipped surface of the stone.
description_view_3: Rear view showing the broad, angular headdress and shoulders, with significant surface erosion and minimal detail.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the entrance to driveways, historical sites, or along urban street corners to protect structures from vehicle impact and demarcate boundaries.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be fully above ground, standing upright on its base, sometimes partially embedded for stability.
attribute_wear_patterns: Visible chipping and erosion on the edges and corners, especially on the front figure and base, likely caused by vehicle contact, weathering, and occasional foot traffic. Surface discoloration and smoothing are present on protruding areas due to repeated contact and environmental exposure.
